claims.claim: In a synthetic, GB10-calibrated analytic simulator spanning 80 scenarios (70B-class target, 3B draft), the governor reduces OOM-risk scenarios from 32 (fixed $k{=}8$) to 24, while winning 36 of 48 comparable scenarios with an average relative throughput gain of 44.65% and zero losses.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Speculative decoding reduces per-token latency in autoregressive language model serving.

evidence_refs.quote: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive inference by drafting candidate tokens with a smaller model and verifying them against the target. In long-context serving, however, the key-value (KV) caches of both target and draft models compete for the same finite GPU memory budget. A fixed speculative depth $k$ can push the system into out-of-memory (OOM) risk under high context lengths, or waste compute on low-acceptance speculation when pressure is low. We propose a KV-pressure speculation

claims.claim: This paper introduces a **KV-pressure speculation governor**: a runtime policy that adjusts speculative depth as a function of (1) projected KV memory usage relative to the available budget and (2) an EMA of the draft acceptance rate.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A negative result: for n-gram speculation, the governor does not improve OOM-risk reduction, though it may provide value in throttling low-acceptance waste—a benefit that remains unquantified.
